dataset.output:
Describing the input grid: The input contains exactly two non-black single cells on an otherwise black grid; their border positions indicate whether they are column clues or row clues.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: If the clues are on the top/bottom borders, turn them into full vertical columns; if the clues are on the left/right borders, turn them into full horizontal rows. Use the distance between the two clue positions along that chosen axis as the spacing, alternate the two clue colors, and continue the repeating stripes only in the positive direction from the earlier clue position.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular black grid with two colored seed cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the exact same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: If the grid is wider than it is tall, use the seed columns to create vertical stripes; if the grid is taller than it is wide, use the seed rows to create horizontal stripes. Sort the two seed positions along the chosen axis, preserve their colors in that order, fill the whole corresponding row or column, then repeat the two-color pattern with the same spacing until the grid edge is reached.

Describing the input grid: The input contains four non-black patterned rectangular panels separated by all-black horizontal and vertical gaps; each panel uses exactly one non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding rectangle of the one panel whose color is different from the other three panels.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the four separated panels, find the non-black color used in each panel, select the panel whose color occurs in only one panel, and output that panel unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several non-black colors on a black background, with one non-black color appearing much less often overall than the other non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding box around all cells of the least frequent non-black color.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of cells of each non-black color, choose the color with the smallest total count, crop the smallest rectangle containing all cells of that color, and preserve the black cells inside that crop.

Describing the input grid: The input is divided by black separator bands into a 2x2 arrangement of patterned panels; the two top panels share the same non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the selected bottom panel.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the shared color of the two top panels as the reference color, then look at the two bottom panels and output the bottom panel whose non-black color is different from that reference color.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid made from repeated rows of non-black colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the palette table [[1,2,3,8],[5,6,4,9]]. For each cell color, find it in the table and replace it with the color directly above or below it in the same column; leave the cell location unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid where each column is a solid vertical stripe, and the same 3-color row is repeated three times.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep every cell in the same position, but replace colors using fixed swap pairs: 1↔5, 2↔6, 3↔4, and 8↔9.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid whose visible objects are three full-height monochrome vertical bars.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Recolor each vertical bar to its paired counterpart while preserving the bar’s shape and column position: blue becomes grey, red becomes fuchsia, green becomes yellow, yellow becomes green, grey becomes blue, fuchsia becomes red, teal becomes brown, and brown becomes teal.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large colored periodic pattern with some cells erased to black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat black cells as missing data. Find the smallest vertical and horizontal repeating period that makes all non-black cells consistent with one repeating tile. Then fill every black cell with the color from the corresponding position in that repeated tile.

Describing the input grid: The input is a set of repeated horizontal row patterns, with some entries in some rows erased to black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Group rows that are compatible, meaning that every column where both rows are non-black has the same value. For each row group, merge the non-black values to reconstruct the full row template. Replace every row by its completed template, filling the black cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a set of repeated vertical column patterns, with some entries in some columns erased to black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Group columns that are compatible, meaning that every row where both columns are non-black has the same value. For each column group, merge the non-black values to reconstruct the full column template. Replace every column by its completed template, filling the black cells.

Describing the input grid: The input contains colored point markers indicating selected rows and columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use red 2 markers as column selectors and color those full columns red. Use all non-red markers, blue 1 and green 3, as row selectors and color those full rows with the marker’s color. Row selectors have priority over column selectors at intersections.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black background with single-cell markers whose colors are numbers.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Odd-colored markers, here 1 and 3, expand horizontally across their whole row. Even-colored markers, here 2, expand vertically across their whole column. When an odd horizontal line and an even vertical line overlap, keep the odd horizontal color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly black grid containing isolated colored cells: blue 1, red 2, and green 3.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every red 2 cell, fill its entire column with 2. For every blue 1 or green 3 cell, fill its entire row with that color. If a horizontal row crosses a red vertical column, the horizontal row color replaces the red at that cell.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains many scattered colors, but one color forms the largest connected hollow rectangle made of straight horizontal and vertical border lines.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the dimensions of the hollow interior of that connected rectangle.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate the bounding box of the hollow rectangular component, remove the component’s outer border, and crop the grid to the remaining interior area.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one complete, axis-aligned, single-color rectangular frame embedded in a larger noisy grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the height and width of that rectangle minus its border: frame height - 2 by frame width - 2.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the monochrome rectangular outline, ignore the outline itself, and output exactly the cells strictly inside the rectangle, preserving their colors and positions.

Describing the input grid: The input grid includes a pair of same-colored horizontal line segments of equal length, vertically aligned, with matching same-colored vertical sides connecting their endpoints.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid spans the rows between the two horizontal segments and the columns between the two vertical sides.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use those four aligned line segments as a rectangular delimiter, then extract the subgrid between them, excluding the delimiter cells themselves.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large black grid containing many scattered distractor colored cells and one larger connected colored object.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding box around the largest 4-connected non-black component.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find all 4-connected components of non-black cells, choose the component with the most cells, remove everything else, and output only its tight bounding rectangle, preserving its color and any black holes inside the rectangle.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large black grid containing several non-black colors, where one color forms the target object and the other colors are scattered noise.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding box around all cells of the least frequent non-black color.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the total number of cells of each non-black color, select the color with the smallest count, discard all other colors, and crop to the smallest rectangle containing every cell of the selected color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large black grid with one compact colored figure and other colors spread sparsely across a much larger area.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the bounding rectangle of the non-black color whose cells occupy the smallest bounding-box area.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each non-black color, compute the tight bounding box containing all cells of that color; choose the color with the smallest bounding-box area, then output that cropped box with the selected color preserved and all other colors treated as black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 black grid containing several pairs of same-colored cells; each pair lies on a 45-degree diagonal.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 10x10 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each color, find its two cells and draw the straight diagonal line segment connecting them, filling every black cell between them with that same color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid where each nonzero color appears exactly twice, and the two occurrences are opposite corners of a square or diagonal rectangle.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each repeated color, take the smallest bounding box containing its two cells, then color the diagonal of that box whose endpoints are the two original cells, using that repeated color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with isolated colored markers placed so that markers of the same color form an incomplete diagonal sequence.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 10x10.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each color, complete the arithmetic sequence between its two markers: move one row at a time from one marker to the other, changing the column by +1 or -1 as needed, and color every visited cell with that color.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains colored patterns where each shape is horizontally symmetric around the center of its bounding box.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each color separately, find its bounding box and vertical symmetry axis. On every row of that bounding box, whenever two same-colored cells appear as a symmetric pair on opposite sides of the axis, replace that pair with a solid horizontal bar of the same color spanning from the left cell to the right cell. Leave background cells outside those spans black.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one or more colored V-shaped or wedge-shaped outlines on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if there are two cells of the same non-black color in that row, fill every black cell horizontally between those two colored cells with that same color. Leave all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one or more colored outline shapes, each forming the two slanted sides and narrow base of a triangular/trapezoidal region.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored outline, fill the interior of the implied shape using the same color. The filled region is obtained by scanline filling: on each row occupied by the outline, color every cell between the left boundary and right boundary of that outline.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing pairs of colored markers along the two vertical borders.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the occurrences of each non-black color. If a color appears exactly twice and those two cells are on opposite ends of the same row, draw a horizontal line of that color across that row from one marker to the other. Otherwise leave the grid unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with colored cells placed only on the far left and far right edges, with black cells elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if the leftmost cell and rightmost cell are the same non-black color, fill the entire row with that color. Leave all other rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with isolated colored boundary cells and empty black space between them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Look for rows that are horizontally symmetric because they have identical non-black colors at the two outermost columns. For each such symmetric row, replace every black cell between the two matching edge cells with that same color. Rows without this matching left-right symmetry stay unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains black background cells, teal cells, and exactly two red 2x2 blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 1x1.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat teal cells as path cells using 4-directional adjacency. If any single connected teal component touches both red 2x2 blocks orthogonally, output [[8]]. Otherwise output [[0]].

Describing the input grid: The grid contains two red 2x2 blocks and multiple teal objects, where each teal object is a 4-connected component of teal cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 1x1.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each teal object, count how many of the two red blocks it is orthogonally adjacent to. If the maximum count is 2, output [[8]]. If every teal object is adjacent to at most one red block, output [[0]].

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains black background cells and some teal cells acting as markers.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all teal cells unchanged. For every pair of teal cells that lie in the same row or the same column, draw a straight green line through the black cells strictly between them. Do not draw anything between diagonal teal cells.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains scattered teal points on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Look separately at each row and each column. If a row or column contains exactly two teal cells, fill every black cell between those two teal cells with green. Rows or columns with fewer than two teal cells, or with more than two teal cells, are left unchanged except for any fills caused by the other direction.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains teal markers, sometimes forming horizontal or vertical alignments.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid size is unchanged.
Describing how to transform the grid: Preserve the teal markers. In each row, sort the teal markers from left to right and fill the black cells between each consecutive pair with green. In each column, sort the teal markers from top to bottom and fill the black cells between each consecutive pair with green. Do not connect markers unless they share a row or column.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing possible horizontal solid bars.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect every horizontal run of 3 identical non-black cells. Copy the positions of those full-width horizontal runs into the output as grey cells, and turn every other cell black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if all 3 cells in that row have the same color, make the entire corresponding output row grey. Otherwise, make the entire corresponding output row black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of numbers where rows may contain either one repeated value or multiple different values.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, count the number of distinct values in that row. If the row has exactly 1 distinct value, output grey in all 3 cells of that row; if it has more than 1 distinct value, output black in all 3 cells of that row.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing one colored object made of connected non-black cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Translate the entire colored object one cell downward without changing its shape, orientation, or color; leave black background everywhere else.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing some non-black cells of a single color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Move every non-black cell down by exactly one row, keeping its column and color unchanged; cells moved from row 3 would fall off the grid, and all other cells become black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid with colored cells only in the upper two rows and an empty black bottom row.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Delete the top row, shift the remaining two rows upward-to-downward order into rows 1 and 2 as black then original row 1, and place original row 2 into row 3; equivalently, insert a new all-black row at the top and drop the original bottom row.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a solid rectangular block, a uniform background color, and several isolated single-cell markers of another color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each isolated marker cell, check whether it lies exactly horizontally or vertically aligned with the rectangle: its row must pass through the rectangle, or its column must pass through the rectangle. If so, draw a straight line of the marker’s color from that marker to the nearest edge of the rectangle, filling only the background cells between them and leaving the rectangle unchanged. Markers not aligned with the rectangle are left unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one large solid rectangle and some scattered single cells whose color is different from both the rectangle and the background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Look at every row and column. If a row contains both rectangle cells and one or more scattered marker cells, fill the background cells between the rectangle and each such marker with the marker color. If a column contains both rectangle cells and one or more scattered marker cells, fill the background cells between the rectangle and each such marker with the marker color. Rows or columns that do not contain part of the rectangle are not extended.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a colored rectangular obstacle on a plain background, plus isolated colored endpoint pixels.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: From each side of the rectangle, cast straight horizontal or vertical rays outward through background cells. Whenever such a ray encounters an isolated endpoint pixel before hitting the grid boundary, recolor all background cells along that ray segment between the rectangle and the endpoint using the endpoint’s color. Endpoint pixels that cannot be reached by a straight ray from any side of the rectangle are not connected.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a top 6x5 red pattern and a bottom 6x5 red pattern, with a yellow divider row between them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6 rows by 5 columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the bottom pattern directly on top of the upper pattern. Any red cells that overlap cancel out, and any red cells that do not overlap remain. Recolor the remaining red cells green, leaving all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 6x5 red-and-black patterns, separated by a horizontal yellow line.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x5, matching the size of each red-and-black pattern.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the top and bottom patterns cell by cell. If exactly one of the two corresponding cells is red, make the output cell green. If both are black or both are red, make the output cell black.

Describing the input grid: The input is split by the yellow row into two equal 6x5 binary masks, where red cells are filled and black cells are empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as one mask, 6x5.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each position, count how many of the two masks have a red cell there. If the count is odd, place a green cell in the output. If the count is even, place a black cell.

Describing the input grid: The input contains isolated colored cells along the top edge of an otherwise black grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each top-row colored cell as the center of a vertical alternating reflection pattern. In rows 0, 2, 4, and so on, copy the color at the center column. In rows 1, 3, 5, and so on, copy the color symmetrically into the two neighboring columns around that center, leaving the center black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid where the first row contains one or more colored markers and all lower rows are black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the first row unchanged. Create a second-row pattern by shifting every colored cell from the first row one step left and one step right, preserving its color and ignoring shifts outside the grid. Then repeat these two rows alternately down the entire grid.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly black grid whose only colored cells are in the top row.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored cell in the top row, extend its color downward in a repeating two-row zigzag pattern: on even-numbered rows place the color in the original column, and on odd-numbered rows place the color one column to the left and one column to the right, if those columns exist.

Describing the input grid: A square grid contains a single colored column on the far left, and the number of rows equals the number of columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input, then for each row r except the last row, place a red cell in column n-1-r; finally, fill every cell in the last row to the right of the original left column with yellow.

Describing the input grid: A square grid of size n by n has a solid vertical line of one non-black color in the leftmost column, with all other cells black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same n by n size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the original left vertical line unchanged, then complete a right-triangle outline by drawing a yellow horizontal line across the bottom row from column 1 to column n-1, and drawing a red diagonal from the top-right corner down-left to the cell just above the bottom-left corner.

Describing the input grid: The input contains four same-colored marker cells forming the corners of an axis-aligned rectangle, with another colored pattern inside that rectangle.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output height is the number of rows strictly between the top and bottom marker rows, and the output width is the number of columns strictly between the left and right marker columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the area strictly inside the four markers; every non-black cell in that cropped area becomes the marker color, and every black cell remains black.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a colored object placed inside a rectangular “window” whose four corners are marked by identical colored pixels; the window border itself is otherwise not drawn.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the interior window size, excluding the rows and columns occupied by the four corner markers.
Describing how to transform the grid: Remove everything outside the marked window and remove the corner markers themselves; keep the enclosed object’s exact relative pattern, but replace its original color with the corner-marker color.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more blue container-like outlines, each with a single non-blue colored cell inside it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored cell inside a blue outline, use that cell’s color to fill the empty interior of its blue container: fill all black cells between the left and right blue walls on rows above the bottom wall, fill the opening in the top blue row, and draw a horizontal line of the same color one row above the container spanning the full container width. Keep all blue cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input shows one or more blue “cup” objects, each paired with a different colored seed cell placed in the hollow part of the cup.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each seed, identify the blue cup containing it. Starting from the seed color, paint a solid rising plume inside the cup: on each non-bottom row of the cup, paint all black cells horizontally between the two blue side walls; on the top row this paints only the central gap between the blue top segments. Then paint a full-width colored bar directly above the cup, aligned with the cup’s left and right blue edges.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains grey connected components with black background cells, including some black regions trapped inside grey components.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every enclosed black connected component, count its cells. If the number of cells is a perfect square, recolor that entire enclosed component red. Otherwise leave it black. All grey cells remain grey.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains grey shapes on a black background, where some grey shapes enclose black holes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find each connected black region that is completely enclosed by grey cells. If that enclosed black region forms a solid square, recolor all cells of that region red. Leave non-square or irregular enclosed black regions unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input grid shows separate grey objects, each fitting inside an axis-aligned bounding rectangle, with some black cells missing from inside the object.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each grey object, take its bounding box. If the bounding box is square and the black cells inside that box form a single missing block whose cell count is a perfect square, recolor those missing cells red. Leave grey objects with non-square bounding boxes, non-square missing counts, or irregular missing interiors unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A 10x10 grid where the meaningful information is stored in the bottom-right cell of each 2x2 region.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 20x20.
Describing how to transform the grid: Sample only the even-numbered rows and even-numbered columns of the input to form a 5x5 miniature grid. Then upscale that 5x5 grid by replacing each sampled cell with a solid 4x4 block of the same color.

Describing the input grid: A 10x10 grid with isolated colored cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 20x20.
Describing how to transform the grid: Divide the input into non-overlapping 2x2 blocks, making a 5x5 block grid. For each 2x2 input block, if it contains a colored cell, fill the corresponding 4x4 block in the output with that color; if it contains no colored cell, fill the corresponding output block with black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with multiple colored 3x3 patterns and a single grey cell acting as a pointer.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate the grey cell. Take the 3x3 region whose top-middle cell is immediately below the grey cell: the rows are the three rows below the grey cell, and the columns are one left of, equal to, and one right of the grey cell’s column. Output that 3x3 region.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 2x2 green square with a red motif positioned diagonally next to it, usually in the upper-left quadrant, though some reflected copies may already be present.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the red motif nearest the upper-left of the green square as the template. Place that motif in all four diagonal regions around the green square: original in the upper-left, horizontally reflected in the upper-right, vertically reflected in the lower-left, and reflected both horizontally and vertically in the lower-right. Preserve any copies already present.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a black background, a 2x2 green block, and one or more red patterns placed in quadrant positions around that green block.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the 2x2 green block as the center mirror. For every red cell, add its horizontal mirror across the green block, its vertical mirror across the green block, and its mirror across both axes. Keep the green block unchanged and leave all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input shows red cells arranged around a central 2x2 green block, with some of the four symmetric red quadrants possibly missing.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Complete the red arrangement so that the set of red cells is symmetric under reflection across the vertical midline of the green block and reflection across the horizontal midline of the green block. Add only the missing red cells required by these two symmetries, keep the green cells fixed, and keep the background black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a stack of solid colored horizontal stripes starting from the top edge, with the rest of the grid black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the top stack of colored rows to the bottom edge, but reverse the row order so that the topmost colored row becomes the bottommost row, the second top row becomes the second bottom row, and so on.

Describing the input grid: The input is a tall grid with one or more complete colored horizontal rows at the top, followed by black rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the input unchanged, then reflect every non-black cell across the horizontal middle of the whole grid, placing a mirrored copy at the corresponding row from the bottom.

Describing the input grid: The input shows the beginning of a vertically symmetric stripe pattern: colored full-width rows at the top and blank space below.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Complete the grid so its row sequence is symmetric from top to bottom: for each colored row near the top, put an identical full-width row in the matching position counted from the bottom, leaving all middle rows black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains colored regions whose ordering can be read cleanly from one outer border of the grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a row if the useful border is the top edge, or a column if the useful border is the left edge; its length is the number of color runs on that edge after compression.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the top row and the left column. Choose the one whose compressed sequence of consecutive color runs contains all distinct colors of the grid. Compress adjacent repeated colors on that border, then output the resulting color sequence in the same orientation as that border.

Describing the input grid: The input is a colored grid where the relevant colors first appear in a consistent sequence when read from the top-left.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output contains each distinct input color exactly once; make it a row if the input has more columns than rows, otherwise make it a column.
Describing how to transform the grid: Scan the input in normal reading order, row by row from left to right. Record each new color the first time it appears, ignoring later repeats. Place those recorded colors in the output in that order.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid filled with several non-black colors forming broad jagged bands.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a one-cell-thick line whose length equals the number of distinct colors in the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the large color bands and order them by their spatial position: left-to-right if the bands are vertical, or top-to-bottom if the bands are horizontal. Output the band colors in that order, using a horizontal row for vertical bands and a vertical column for horizontal bands.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a small symmetric 3x3 X-shaped object and another larger colored shape located in one quadrant around it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the center of the X-shaped object as an origin. For each cell of the other colored shape, compute its row and column offset from that center. Place that color at all four positions formed by independently changing the signs of the row offset and column offset. Leave the X-shaped object in place.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two colored objects on a black background: one 5-cell X-shaped marker object, and one separate colored pattern object.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the center cell of the X-shaped marker. Keep the marker unchanged. Reflect every cell of the other colored object across the vertical line through the marker center, across the horizontal line through the marker center, and across both lines, keeping the original object too.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a colored pattern near a 5-cell X marker, with empty black space where matching copies could fit around the marker.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the original input dimensions.
Describing how to transform the grid: Complete the scene so the non-marker colored pattern has mirror symmetry both left-right and up-down about the center of the X marker. Copy the pattern into the other three corresponding quadrants, using horizontal flip, vertical flip, and both flips respectively, while preserving the marker color and position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more solid monochrome rectangles on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each solid colored rectangle, keep its outer one-cell-thick border in its original color, and recolor every strictly interior cell of the rectangle to teal 8. Leave black cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains solid colored blocks made from repeated same-color cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input, then find every 3x3 subgrid that is entirely filled with one nonblack color. For each such 3x3 subgrid, recolor its center cell to teal 8. All other cells keep their original values.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black background containing connected regions of nonblack colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input, then scan every nonblack cell. If the cell directly above, below, left, and right of it all exist and have the same color as that cell in the input, change that cell to teal 8. Otherwise leave the cell unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains colored cells and black cells, including black “gap” cells that are surrounded on the left, right, and above by cells of the same non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input grid unchanged, then find every black cell whose immediate left, immediate right, and immediate upper neighbors are all the same non-black color. For each such black gap cell, place a yellow cell in the bottom row in the same column.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one or more connected non-black objects, each with a 2x3 bounding box and a horizontally centered column.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input grid unchanged, then for each connected non-black object, compute the center column of its bounding box and put a yellow cell in the last row at that column.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one or more small connected colored objects shaped like a 2-row by 3-column arch: three colored cells on the top row and two colored cells below the left and right ends.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input grid unchanged, then for each arch-shaped object, place a yellow cell in the bottom row of the grid, aligned with the middle column of that object’s 3-column width.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a blue 2x2 object and a red 2x2 object placed at different positions in a black 10x10 grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For the blue object, repeatedly copy its top-left cell one step up and one step left, continuing until leaving the grid. For the red object, repeatedly copy its bottom-right cell one step down and one step right, continuing until leaving the grid. All original colored cells stay in place.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 black grid containing one 2x2 blue block and one 2x2 red block.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 10x10 size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep both 2x2 blocks unchanged. From the upper-left corner of the blue block, draw a blue diagonal ray upward-left until it reaches the grid edge. From the lower-right corner of the red block, draw a red diagonal ray downward-right until it reaches the grid edge.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with exactly two solid 2x2 colored squares, one using color 1 and one using color 2, on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 10x10.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each square as lying on a northwest-to-southeast diagonal. For the color-1 square, complete that diagonal only on the northwest side of the square, coloring the added cells 1. For the color-2 square, complete that diagonal only on the southeast side of the square, coloring the added cells 2.

Describing the input grid: The input contains three same-colored single cells lying on one diagonal, equally spaced; the middle of the three cells is the center.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Let the spacing between neighboring colored cells be `d` rows and `d` columns. Draw axis-aligned square outlines centered on the middle colored cell, with half-side lengths `d, 2d, 3d, ...`, clipping any parts that fall outside the grid. Also keep the center cell colored. Use the same color as the input dots; all other cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with exactly three non-black cells of one color, forming a diagonal arithmetic progression.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the central colored cell and call its row and column `(cr, cc)`. Let `d` be the absolute row difference between adjacent input dots. For every cell `(r, c)`, compute `m = max(abs(r-cr), abs(c-cc))`. Color the cell with the input color if `m` is `0` or a positive multiple of `d`; otherwise make it black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains separated groups of yellow cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each yellow group, identify the rows and columns occupied by that group. Any black cell whose row and column are both occupied by that same group is changed to orange, while the original yellow cells remain yellow and all other black cells stay black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing two separated yellow shapes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each connected yellow shape, find its smallest enclosing rectangle. Keep the existing yellow cells unchanged, and recolor every black cell inside that rectangle orange.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with yellow cells forming incomplete 3x3 blocks in separate locations.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each separated yellow cluster as belonging to its own 3x3 tile. Within each such 3x3 tile, keep yellow cells yellow and change all remaining black cells in that tile to orange; leave all cells outside those tiles black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single colored point on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the original dimensions.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every cell in the grid, compare its coordinates to the coordinates of the colored input point. Color the cell with the same color if it has either the same row-minus-column value or the same row-plus-column value as the input point; otherwise leave it black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with one non-black marker cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the marker cell as the center of nested square outlines. For every possible square radius, color the four diagonal corner positions around the marker, if they are still inside the grid. This produces colored cells at all positions offset by equal row and column distances from the marker.

Describing the input grid: The input is an otherwise black grid containing exactly one colored cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the same color as the single input cell, and draw the two complete diagonal lines that pass through that cell: one northwest-to-southeast diagonal and one northeast-to-southwest diagonal, stopping at the grid borders.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains multiple colored connected components, with black as background and grey as a special replacement color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Ignore the grey object and examine the normalized shape of every other colored connected component. Find the pair of components with identical normalized cell patterns in the same orientation. Recolor the later-occurring member of that duplicate pair, scanning from top to bottom and left to right, to grey. Leave everything else unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a teal-framed 3-by-3 set of compartments, each compartment being 3x3 and containing black cells plus one or more fuchsia cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3, representing the nine compartments.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each compartment, count the fuchsia cells. If the count is even, color the corresponding output cell blue; if the count is odd, color it black.

Describing the input grid: The input is an 11x11 grid divided by teal separator lines into nine 3x3 compartments; the meaningful cells inside the compartments are fuchsia dots on black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3, with one cell corresponding to each 3x3 compartment of the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of fuchsia cells in each 3x3 compartment. If a compartment contains exactly two fuchsia cells, put a blue cell in the corresponding output position; otherwise put black.

Describing the input grid: The input is an 11x11 grid split into a 3x3 array of smaller 3x3 regions by teal grid lines, with fuchsia marks scattered inside those regions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 3x3 region, count its fuchsia cells. Find the largest fuchsia-cell count among all nine regions, and mark every region with that largest count as blue in the output; all other output cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains nonzero cells forming a full horizontal stripe and a full vertical stripe, with the two stripes meeting at one shared position.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the row of the horizontal stripe and the column of the vertical stripe. For every cell whose row is within 1 of that stripe row and whose column is within 1 of that stripe column, change it to yellow, except for the exact intersection cell, which keeps its original color. All other cells remain as in the input.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains two perpendicular one-cell-thick colored lines, one horizontal and one vertical, crossing at a single cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the crossing point of the horizontal and vertical lines. Color the eight surrounding cells in the 3x3 neighborhood centered on that crossing point yellow, while leaving the crossing cell itself unchanged and leaving all cells outside that 3x3 neighborhood unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The grid shows a cross-like figure made from two different colored straight bars on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate the unique non-black cell that has colored cells continuing horizontally and vertically through it. Stamp a 3x3 yellow square centered on that cell, then restore only the center cell to its original input color; everything outside the stamped square is copied unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 20x20 grid containing black cells and one other non-black color used as noise/background texture.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 20x20 size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find each 3x3 block made entirely of black cells. Scan possible 3x3 blocks from top to bottom and left to right; whenever an all-black 3x3 block is found, recolor those 9 cells blue, and ignore later 3x3 candidates that overlap cells already recolored blue. Leave all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 20x20 grid of black cells mixed with a single non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 20x20.
Describing how to transform the grid: Locate clusters of overlapping all-black 3x3 square candidates. For each cluster of one or more overlapping 3x3 black squares, choose the candidate whose top-left corner is highest, breaking ties by choosing the leftmost. Recolor that chosen 3x3 square blue. Leave every other cell unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a noisy 20x20 grid where hidden solid black 3x3 square regions appear among cells of one foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify maximal empty square regions of size 3x3: a valid region is a 3x3 square of black cells that is not simply a down-right shifted overlap of another all-black 3x3 square starting one row above and one column to the left. Recolor each valid 3x3 black region blue, preserving all other black and colored cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing non-black colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of distinct colors in the input. If there is 1 distinct color, draw a grey horizontal line across the top row. If there are 2 distinct colors, draw a grey main diagonal from top-left to bottom-right. If there are 3 distinct colors, draw a grey anti-diagonal from top-right to bottom-left. All other cells become black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the three possible lines: the top row, the main diagonal, and the anti-diagonal. For each line, count how many distinct colors appear on that line in the input. Output grey cells on the line with the highest distinct-color count. If all three lines have only one distinct color, choose the top row. If the main diagonal and anti-diagonal tie for the highest count above one, choose the main diagonal. All other cells become black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count how many times each color appears. If the whole grid is one color, output a grey top row. Otherwise, if all present colors occur the same number of times, output a grey anti-diagonal from top-right to bottom-left. In all other cases, output a grey main diagonal from top-left to bottom-right. All non-line cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains examples of symmetric three-cell color triples, where two matching outer colors surround a different center color in a row, column, or diagonal.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Learn every complete A-B-A triple orientation that appears in the input. For each learned triple, complete all matching incomplete triples in the same orientation: if the center B is present alone, add the two outer A cells; if the two outer A cells are present with the center missing, add B; if one side of an A-B-A triple is missing, add the missing A. Multiple learned triples may combine at the same center, producing plus-shapes or X-shapes.

Describing the input grid: A symmetric colored table has been partially covered by black rectangular holes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Restore the holes by symmetry across the main diagonal: any black cell whose transposed partner is non-black takes that transposed color. After doing this, any remaining black cell on the main diagonal takes the diagonal color, and any remaining off-diagonal black cell takes the background/off-diagonal color immediately preceding the diagonal color in the cyclic palette.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a colored rectangle embedded in black; the rectangle has no black cells inside it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same height and width as that colored rectangle.
Describing how to transform the grid: Delete all outer black rows and columns, then copy the remaining rectangle row by row, reading each row from right to left instead of left to right.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a solid rectangle of a main color with a smaller pattern of a second color inside it, all surrounded by black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the same size as the solid colored rectangle, excluding the surrounding black area.
Describing how to transform the grid: Fill the output rectangle with the main color, then place the second-color pattern in the horizontally mirrored column positions within the rectangle, preserving the original row positions.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one non-black rectangular block made from two colors, surrounded by black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the tight bounding rectangle around the non-black block.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop out the non-black rectangle, then reflect that cropped rectangle horizontally, so the left and right columns are swapped while the row order and colors stay the same.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large noisy version of a small color grid: each small-grid cell has been expanded into a larger rectangular block containing mostly one color plus black cells, with black separator lines between blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the underlying small grid, with one cell per expanded block.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compress each expanded block into a single cell by ignoring black cells and taking the color that appears inside that block. Preserve the relative positions of the blocks to form the final compact color grid.

Describing the input grid: The input is a set of rectangular color panels separated by complete black rows and complete black columns; each panel contains one non-black color mixed with black holes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has one row for each horizontal band of panels and one column for each vertical band of panels.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input at every all-black row and all-black column. For each resulting rectangular panel, find its non-black color and place that color in the corresponding position of the smaller output grid.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid containing a blue shape of area 1, 2, 3, or 4 cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Ignore the blue shape’s location and geometry, keep only its area, and replace it with a canonical red shape of the same area: area 1 becomes the top-left cell, area 2 becomes the first two cells of the top row, area 3 becomes the full top row, and area 4 becomes the full top row plus the center cell.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid containing some blue cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of blue cells. Replace them with the same number of red cells placed in a fixed order: top-left, top-middle, top-right, then center.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid where the blue cells act as movable tokens and black cells are empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Scan the input and collect all blue tokens, ignoring their original positions. Re-place the tokens as red cells into the output’s counting track: fill the top row from left to right first, and if there is a fourth token, place it in the center.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single-color maze-like arrangement of vertical and horizontal barriers on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the barrier cells unchanged. Partition the black cells into 4-connected regions separated by the barrier cells. Any black region that touches the outer edge of the grid becomes green 3, while any black region completely surrounded by barriers and not touching the edge becomes red 2.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing a network of one non-black color made from horizontal and vertical line segments.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all original non-black line cells unchanged. Treat those line cells as walls, then flood-fill through black cells from the outside border using 4-directional movement. Change every black cell reachable from the border to green 3, and change every remaining black cell, which is enclosed by the walls, to red 2.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with one colored orthogonal line drawing that forms some complete axis-aligned rectangular chambers and some incomplete/open line structures.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Preserve the original colored line drawing. Find every closed rectangle whose top, bottom, left, and right sides are continuous segments of the original line color. Fill all black cells inside any such closed rectangle with red 2, leaving line cells unchanged, and fill every other black cell with green 3.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a same-colored 2x2 square and extra colored pixels positioned on diagonals aligned with corners of that square.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same height and width as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each extra colored pixel, use its position relative to the 2x2 square to define a one-step diagonal translation: up-left, up-right, down-left, or down-right. Repeatedly copy that pixel by this translation vector, preserving the color, until the next copy would fall outside the grid. All original colored cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing one non-black color, forming a solid 2x2 square plus one or more same-colored single cells diagonally adjacent to the square.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all existing colored cells. Find the 2x2 square. For each same-colored single cell outside the square, determine the diagonal direction going away from the nearest corner of the 2x2 square, then continue drawing that same color in that diagonal direction until the edge of the grid is reached.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with a central 2x2 colored block and partial diagonal branches touching its corners.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 10x10.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the 2x2 block as the center of an X-shaped figure. Any colored cell diagonally outside one of the four corners indicates that that arm of the X should exist. Extend each indicated arm outward from the block along its diagonal path, filling every missing cell of that arm with the same color until the arm hits the grid boundary.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x6.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build the output row by row from the input rows and columns. For output rows 0, 1, and 2, concatenate input row r with input column r read from bottom to top. For output rows 3, 4, and 5, let k = 5-r; concatenate input column k read from top to bottom with input row k read from right to left.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x6.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each input cell at row r and column c, copy its color into four positions in the output by rotating that cell position around the center of the 6x6 grid: place it at (r,c), (c,5-r), (5-c,r), and (5-r,5-c), using zero-based indexing.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid of colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x6.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the output into four 3x3 quadrants. Put the original input in the upper-left quadrant, the input rotated 90 degrees clockwise in the upper-right quadrant, the input rotated 90 degrees counterclockwise in the lower-left quadrant, and the input rotated 180 degrees in the lower-right quadrant.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a grey shape made from 3x3 solid grey blocks inside a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the tight 9x9 bounding box around the grey shape, divide it into a 3x3 grid of 3x3 blocks, and record which blocks are grey to form a 3x3 pattern. Then replace every grey cell of that 3x3 pattern with a copy of the whole 3x3 pattern, and replace every black cell with a 3x3 black block.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single grey block-pattern whose bounding box is 9x9, made of nine possible 3x3 regions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compress the grey shape to a 3x3 binary mask by treating each 3x3 region as one cell. Make one 9x9 mask by enlarging this 3x3 mask with each cell becoming a solid 3x3 block, and make another 9x9 mask by tiling the same 3x3 mask in every 3x3 block position. The output is grey only where both masks are grey.

Describing the input grid: The input shows grey 3x3 squares arranged as a 3x3 macro-grid, surrounded by black space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the occupied macro-cell coordinates in the 3x3 arrangement. For every ordered pair of occupied macro-cells `(r1,c1)` and `(r2,c2)`, color the output cell at row `3*r1+r2` and column `3*c1+c2` grey; all other output cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 3x3 colored pattern made from all non-black, non-grey cells, plus a single grey cell elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the grey cell as the intended center position of a duplicate 3x3 pattern. Paste the same 3x3 pattern with its top-left corner one row above and one column left of the grey cell. Preserve all original colored cells and replace the grey cell with the pasted color at that position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one multicolored non-grey object and one grey marker cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the entire multicolored object without changing its orientation. Place the copy so that the center of the object’s bounding box lands on the grey marker cell. The grey marker is overwritten by the copied object’s corresponding color, while the original object remains unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single fully colored horizontal row, with one patterned colored row directly above it and one patterned colored row directly below it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Use the fully colored row as an anchor. Repeat the fully colored row every 3 rows above and below its original position. Also repeat the row above the anchor every 3 rows, and repeat the row below the anchor every 3 rows, clipping to the grid boundaries.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with one non-black color forming a contiguous 3-row horizontal motif, with empty black rows above and/or below it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extract the 3 non-empty rows as a repeating vertical tile. Fill every row of the output by repeating those 3 rows upward and downward, preserving the original phase: output row `r` is the extracted motif row corresponding to `(r - top_of_motif) mod 3`.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one non-black color forming a 3x3 local pattern, with the rest of the 9x9 grid black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same 9x9 dimensions.
Describing how to transform the grid: Record the local coordinates of every colored cell inside the 3x3 pattern. For every ordered pair of colored cells (a,b) and (c,d), color the output cell at position (3a+c, 3b+d) using the input color; all other cells stay black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing one monochrome 3x3 pattern placed somewhere on the black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the non-black 3x3 pattern. Make a 3x3 array of 3x3 blocks: wherever the cropped pattern has a colored cell, place a full copy of the cropped pattern in the corresponding 3x3 block; wherever it has a black cell, leave that 3x3 block black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid with a single colored shape whose tight bounding box is 3x3.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Convert the 3x3 shape into a binary mask of colored versus black cells. The output cell at row r and column c is colored if both mask[floor(r/3), floor(c/3)] and mask[r mod 3, c mod 3] are colored; otherwise it is black. Use the same color as the input shape.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains black background cells and multiple identical grey 2x2 blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Around each grey 2x2 block, form an implied 4x4 bounding box whose center is the grey block. Fill only the four corners of that 4x4 box: top-left corner becomes 1, top-right becomes 2, bottom-left becomes 3, and bottom-right becomes 4. All other black cells remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input grid contains one or more separate 2x2 grey squares on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep every grey 2x2 square unchanged. For each grey square, place four colored cells diagonally just outside its corners: blue at the upper-left outside corner, red at the upper-right outside corner, green at the lower-left outside corner, and yellow at the lower-right outside corner.

Describing the input grid: The input grid shows isolated 2x2 grey objects, each made of four grey cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each grey object, identify its four grey cells. From the top-left grey cell, move one step up-left and color that cell blue. From the top-right grey cell, move one step up-right and color that cell red. From the bottom-left grey cell, move one step down-left and color that cell green. From the bottom-right grey cell, move one step down-right and color that cell yellow. Keep the original grey cells.

Describing the input grid: A 4x4 blue/black pattern appears above a yellow divider row, and a 4x4 red/black pattern appears below it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 4x4, matching the size of each pattern block.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the upper and lower 4x4 blocks cell by cell. If exactly one of the two corresponding cells is colored, make the output cell green. If both are black or both are colored, make the output cell black.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two aligned 4x4 binary masks separated by a yellow row: the upper mask uses blue for filled cells, and the lower mask uses red for filled cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 4 rows by 4 columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat blue and red cells as filled and black cells as empty. Add the two aligned masks using parity: a cell becomes green if the total number of filled cells in that position across the two masks is odd, and black if the total is even.

Describing the input grid: There are two same-sized colored shapes, one blue above the yellow separator and one red below it, each occupying positions in a 4x4 area.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as one of the shape areas, 4x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the lower red shape onto the upper blue shape in the same orientation. Any position where the two shapes overlap disappears, and any position where neither shape exists stays black. All remaining non-overlapping shape cells are recolored green.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing one or more isolated colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored cell, draw an L-shape in that same color: extend horizontally from the cell to the right edge, then extend vertically downward along the rightmost column to the bottom edge. If L-shapes overlap, cells from lower starting points overwrite cells from higher starting points.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing sparse colored cells that act as row-start signals.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the input dimensions.
Describing how to transform the grid: Scan rows from top to bottom. When a row contains a colored cell, fill that row from the colored cell through the rightmost column with that color, and set the current active color to that color. For rows below it until another colored cell appears, fill only the rightmost column with the current active color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with isolated colored marker cells, each marker being on its own row.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Project each colored marker horizontally to the right border, filling every cell from the marker to the right edge in that marker’s color. Then connect the projected points on the right border vertically: each right-border segment takes the color of the marker immediately above it, continuing until the next marker row or the bottom of the grid.

Describing the input grid: A blue-background grid containing a compact hidden pattern made from colors other than blue.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is obtained by removing every all-blue border row and all-blue border column from the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Repeatedly trim rows from the top and bottom and columns from the left and right as long as they contain only 1s; then replace all remaining 1s with 0s while leaving all other colors unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A grid with blue cells acting as the background and several non-blue colored cells embedded in it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the smallest rectangle that contains every non-blue cell from the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the input to the bounding box of all cells whose value is not 1, preserve those non-blue colors, and change any remaining blue cells inside the crop to black.

Describing the input grid: A set of colored non-blue cells placed on a blue empty field.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has height equal to the vertical span of the non-blue cells and width equal to the horizontal span of the non-blue cells.
Describing how to transform the grid: Translate all non-blue cells together so that the topmost occupied row and leftmost occupied column become the top and left edges of the output, keep their colors and relative positions, and fill all unoccupied positions with black.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a grey container with a rectangular teal liquid region at its bottom and black space above it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is always 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Measure the vertical depth of the liquid surface from the container opening: subtract the row index of the top grey wall from the row index of the topmost teal cell. Draw that many teal cells in unary on the 3x3 grid, using the order top-left, top-middle, top-right, middle-right, middle-middle, middle-left, bottom-left, bottom-middle, bottom-right; all unused cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input grid shows green rectangular blocks on a black background, with each block having exactly one red cell embedded on its boundary.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each red cell as the start of a “laser beam” pointing directly away from the center of its containing rectangle, perpendicular to the rectangle side it lies on. Draw that beam to the outer border of the grid. Then thicken the beam equally on both sides by a radius equal to the distance from the red cell to the opposite side of its rectangle. Color the center beam red and the thickened surrounding cells green, without altering the original block.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing grey cells forming horizontal and vertical runs inside rectangular regions.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each grey cell, check whether it is at an endpoint of its horizontal grey run and whether it is at an endpoint of its vertical grey run. If it is both a horizontal-run endpoint and a vertical-run endpoint, color it blue. If it is exactly one of those, color it yellow. If it is neither, color it red. Leave black cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid containing one or more solid grey rectangular objects.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each grey rectangle, recolor its four corner cells blue, its non-corner border cells yellow, and its interior cells red. Leave all black cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with grey cells arranged into solid blocks.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every grey cell, count its grey orthogonal neighbors up, down, left, and right. If it has 2 grey orthogonal neighbors, make it blue; if it has 3, make it yellow; if it has 4, make it red. Leave black cells black.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains teal barrier cells, black open cells, and a few pre-colored cells using exactly two non-teal colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
