Describing how to transform the grid: Treat every non-teal cell as open space and find the 4-connected open component containing the pre-colored cells. The colored cells define a checkerboard coloring by row-plus-column parity: one clue color belongs to one parity and the other clue color belongs to the opposite parity. Fill every black cell in that same component according to this parity rule, while leaving all other open components black and keeping teal cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a two-color object resting near the bottom; its uppermost non-black row is a horizontal run or single cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all existing cells. Find the left and right ends of the uppermost non-black horizontal run, then draw 45-degree diagonal rays upward-left from the left end and upward-right from the right end. Color the added cells with the contrasting color that appears in the middle of the bottom row of the object.

Describing the input grid: The input shows the lower part of a symmetric “roof” or triangular outline: a colored base object is present, but its two sloping upper sides are missing.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Preserve the given object, then complete the missing symmetric sloped outline above it. The completed outline consists of two diagonal lines that expand outward by one column for each row upward, starting from the two outer corners of the object’s top colored row. Use the color of the object’s lower central segment for these new diagonal cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 5x7 grid containing one non-black color, with four possible 2x2 corner regions separated by black space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the four 2x2 corner regions as overlapping crops of one 3x3 image: the upper-left 2x2 maps to output rows 0-1 and columns 0-1, the upper-right 2x2 maps to rows 0-1 and columns 1-2, the lower-left 2x2 maps to rows 1-2 and columns 0-1, and the lower-right 2x2 maps to rows 1-2 and columns 1-2. Overlay them; a cell in the output is colored if any contributing input cell is colored, otherwise it is black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 5x7 grid where the middle row and middle vertical band act as spacing between colored edge/corner cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Collapse the input by merging row groups [0], [1,3], [4] into output rows 0, 1, and 2, and merging column groups [0], [1,5], [6] into output columns 0, 1, and 2. For each output cell, if any input cell in the corresponding row-group and column-group is colored, place that color there; otherwise place black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 5x7 grid with colored cells only in the outer two columns on each side, using a single non-black color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build each output cell from fixed input positions: output corners copy the four extreme input corners, output edge-centers are the OR/union of the two input cells on the matching separated edge, and the center is the OR/union of the four inner corner cells adjacent to the central gap. More explicitly, use input positions (0,0) for output (0,0), (0,1) or (0,5) for (0,1), (0,6) for (0,2), (1,0) or (3,0) for (1,0), (1,1) or (1,5) or (3,1) or (3,5) for (1,1), (1,6) or (3,6) for (1,2), (4,0) for (2,0), (4,1) or (4,5) for (2,1), and (4,6) for (2,2).

Describing the input grid: The input contains separate connected grey outline objects on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each grey outline object, count how many grey cells make up the outline. If the outline has 8 grey cells, fill its interior with 6; if it has 12 grey cells, fill its interior with 7; if it has 16 grey cells, fill its interior with 8. Leave all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black background containing one or more grey hollow square frames.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep every grey frame unchanged, and fill the black cells enclosed inside each frame according to the frame’s outer side length: 3x3 frames get color 6, 4x4 frames get color 7, and 5x5 frames get color 8.

Describing the input grid: The input is a black grid with grey closed rectangular/square outlines that enclose black interior cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each closed grey outline, count the number of black cells completely enclosed by it. If it encloses 1 cell, color that cell 6; if it encloses 4 cells, color them 7; if it encloses 9 cells, color them 8. All grey cells and outside black cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains scattered black cells plus some uninterrupted straight black paths spanning the whole grid horizontally or vertically.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is identical in dimensions to the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each black cell, check whether its entire row is black or its entire column is black in the original input. If yes, replace that cell with red 2. Otherwise keep the cell unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a colored pattern divided into rectangular regions by one-cell-wide black grid lines.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the black divider lines that run completely from one edge of the grid to the opposite edge, either horizontally or vertically. Change those divider-line cells to red 2, while preserving all non-divider cells exactly as they are.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid containing one main nonzero color, black background cells, and some complete black separator lines.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find every row and every column that is entirely black in the input. Recolor all cells in those full-black rows and columns to red 2. Leave every other cell unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A rectangular grid containing blue 1, teal 8, and orange 7 cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the input grid, but replace every orange 7 cell with grey 5; leave all blue 1 and teal 8 cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A rectangular grid in which the bottom-left cell indicates the target color to be recolored.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the color in the bottom-left cell of the input, then replace every cell of that color with grey 5 while leaving all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A rectangular grid where blue 1 and teal 8 are preserved colors, and any other nonzero color is a marked color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each cell, if it is 1 or 8, keep it unchanged; otherwise replace it with grey 5.

Describing the input grid: The input contains many repeated colors plus one rare color that appears as a connected rectangular outline.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the bounding rectangle of that rare-color outline, here 7x7.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the connected component made from the rare outline color, take its minimum and maximum row and column positions, and copy that entire rectangular subgrid from the input as the output.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large repeated pattern with one 7x7 hollow square frame drawn in a special marker color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the size of that framed region, which is 7x7.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the color that forms a complete one-cell-thick hollow square frame; crop the input to the bounding box of that frame, including the frame and the 5x5 interior.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 12x12 grid containing grey cells and a colored reference column on the far left.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, use the color in the first column as that row’s replacement color. Every grey cell in that same row is changed to the first-column color of the row. Black cells remain black, and the original colored first-column cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 12x12 grid where grey cells form a mask over rows labeled by colors in the first column.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the vertical color pattern from the first column into the grey mask: for every grey cell, look at its row index and replace it with the color appearing in column 0 of that row. All non-grey cells keep their original values.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 12x12 grid with colored cells along the left edge and grey shapes elsewhere.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each colored cell in the leftmost column as projecting its color horizontally across its row. Wherever that horizontal projection passes through a grey cell, recolor that grey cell with the projecting color. Leave black cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid containing several grey single cells on a black background, with each grey cell located at the center of a possible 3x3 region.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid, 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every grey cell with a filled 3x3 blue square centered on that grey cell. All other cells remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid representing an enlarged 3x3 layout, where grey cells indicate selected positions in the smaller 3x3 layout.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is a 9x9 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Convert the grey-cell positions into a 3x3 binary pattern by mapping rows 2, 5, and 8 to small-grid rows 1, 2, and 3, and columns 2, 5, and 8 to small-grid columns 1, 2, and 3. Then upscale each selected small-grid cell into a 3x3 blue block in the output; unselected small-grid cells become black 3x3 blocks.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 9x9 grid that can be divided into nine non-overlapping 3x3 blocks, with some blocks containing a grey marker.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid, 9x9.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 3x3 block, if that block contains at least one grey cell, fill the entire corresponding 3x3 block in the output with blue. If a 3x3 block contains no grey cell, leave it black.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid containing colored cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is also 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Repeatedly scan downward through each column: whenever a black cell has a colored cell directly above it, change that black cell to the same color. Continue this propagation until no more black cells can inherit a color from above.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid with black background cells and a few isolated colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every non-black cell, extend its color straight downward in the same column until the bottom edge of the grid is reached. All cells above or not reached by these downward extensions remain black.

Describing the input grid: A 3x3 grid with several colored pixels and black empty cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same 3x3 dimensions.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the original grid with copies of itself shifted downward by 1 row and by 2 rows, clipping anything that moves outside the grid. Black cells are transparent in the overlay, and colored cells keep their original colors.

Describing the input grid: The input has colored cells only in some corners, acting as sources, with all other cells black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Assign every cell to the active corner source with the smallest Euclidean distance; if two active sources are equally close, output black there. For a cell assigned to a corner, compute the smallest axis-aligned square with that corner as one corner and the cell on its outer edge. If that square has odd side length, fill the cell with the corner’s color; if the side length is even, leave it black.

Describing the input grid: The input is an otherwise black square grid with one or more colored marker cells placed at corners.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each output cell, find the uniquely closest colored corner marker by Manhattan distance; if there is a tie, leave the cell black. For the unique closest marker, measure the cell’s row-offset and column-offset from that marker’s corner. If the larger of those two offsets is even, color the cell with that marker’s color; otherwise leave it black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing a single 2x2 green object and scattered colored dots.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same 10x10 dimensions.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the colored dots as endpoints and the green square as a target. For each colored dot, check whether a purely horizontal or purely vertical segment from that dot would touch the green square without changing row or column. If so, color every black cell on that segment with the dot’s color, stopping at the green square. If not, copy the dot without adding anything.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing one 2x2 green square and several isolated colored pixels of one or two other colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the green square and all original colored pixels. For every non-green colored pixel that lies in the same row or same column as any cell of the green square, draw a straight horizontal or vertical line of that pixel’s color through the black cells between that pixel and the green square. Pixels not row- or column-aligned with the green square do not create lines.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with a 2x2 green block used as an anchor, plus scattered single-cell colored markers.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 10x10.
Describing how to transform the grid: From each side of the green block, look outward along the two rows or two columns occupied by the block. Whenever a colored marker is found directly outward along one of those horizontal or vertical paths, fill the whole gap from the edge of the green block to that marker with the marker’s color. Leave all markers that are not directly visible from the green block in a cardinal direction unchanged.

Describing the input grid: A fixed-height grid contains a left-aligned colored pattern, with the right side mostly empty black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the smallest horizontal period width p such that the already-drawn non-black cells are consistent with repeating the first p columns. Then fill the whole grid by copying columns 0 through p-1 repeatedly from left to right until the full width is filled.

Describing the input grid: Each column of the left portion belongs to a repeating sequence of vertical column-patterns, while the remaining columns are empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each column as a vertical symbol made from its stack of colors. Determine the shortest repeating sequence of these column-symbols visible at the left, then extend that column-symbol sequence across all 15 columns, writing the corresponding colored stacks into the output.

Describing the input grid: The non-black cells form the beginning of a horizontally repeating motif; at least one full copy of the motif and part of the next copy are visible on the left.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the original grid size.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the complete motif as the smallest leftmost rectangular block whose translation to the right matches the next visible part of the pattern. Copy that whole motif repeatedly to the right, preserving all colors and black cells inside the motif, until the row width is filled.

Describing the input grid: The input grid has colored cells only in two side-by-side 3-column regions: yellow cells in columns 1-3 and green cells in columns 4-6.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height as the input and half its width, so it is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each output position, examine the paired input cells in the same row from columns c and c+3; if either paired cell is colored, output 6, and if both are black, output 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3-row by 6-column grid containing a yellow 3x3 pattern on the left and a green 3x3 pattern on the right.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the input into two 3x3 halves, overlay the right half onto the left half, and put fuchsia 6 in every cell where either half has a non-black cell; otherwise put black 0.

Describing the input grid: The input shows two separate 3x3 binary masks, one encoded with color 4 and one encoded with color 3.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the size of one mask, 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat both colored masks as occupied-cell maps, combine their occupied cells into a single map, and recolor every occupied cell fuchsia 6 while leaving only positions that were empty in both masks as black 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing colored reference cells in the top row and grey rectangular connected components in the lower rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the top-row markers and black background unchanged. For each grey connected component, compute its horizontal center column, then recolor the whole component using the color of the top-row marker whose column is closest to that center.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with several single colored marker cells in the top row and several grey rectangular objects below them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all non-grey cells unchanged. For each grey connected object, find the colored marker in the top row whose column passes through that grey object. Recolor the entire grey object with that marker’s color.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with the same number of top-row colored markers as grey connected objects below.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Sort the colored markers from left to right by column, and sort the grey connected objects from left to right by their horizontal center. Recolor the first grey object with the first marker color, the second grey object with the second marker color, and so on, leaving all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one small colored cross-like object on a black background, with one horizontal colored run and one vertical colored run crossing at a shared colored cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same height and width as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the row containing the horizontal colored run and the column containing the vertical colored run. Repeat the horizontal run’s colors periodically left and right across that entire row, preserving the alignment of the original cells. Repeat the vertical run’s colors periodically up and down across that entire column, preserving the alignment of the original cells. Leave all other cells black.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a small sample of two infinite periodic stripes: a horizontal stripe and a vertical stripe, intersecting at one colored cell.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat the visible nonblack cells in the multi-colored horizontal row as one period of a horizontal color cycle, and treat the visible nonblack cells in the multi-colored vertical column as one period of a vertical color cycle. Extend each cycle across the full grid boundary in its own direction, using modular repetition so the given colored cells remain unchanged. All cells not on either completed stripe remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains colored pieces on both sides of a central grey divider, with black as empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the left-side canvas only, so it is 10x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep every colored cell already on the left of the grey divider. Then take every colored cell on the right of the divider and copy it to the mirror-symmetric position on the left side, preserving its row and color. The grey divider and the original right side are discarded.

Describing the input grid: The input has a vertical grey separator column, with a 10x4 colored pattern on its left and a 10x4 colored pattern on its right.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 10x4, matching the height of the input and the width of one side of the separator.
Describing how to transform the grid: Fold the right-hand pattern leftward across the grey separator, so columns on the right are horizontally reflected onto the left side. Remove the grey separator and overlay the reflected right pattern onto the left pattern, keeping all non-black cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is divided by a grey vertical line into four columns on the left and four columns on the right.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has 10 rows and 4 columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, merge each pair of cells equidistant from the grey line: input columns 0 and 8 become output column 0, columns 1 and 7 become output column 1, columns 2 and 6 become output column 2, and columns 3 and 5 become output column 3. If both paired cells are black, output black; otherwise output the non-black color.

Describing the input grid: A 10x10 grey grid with several black “holes” or islands of black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep all grey cells as 5. For every 4-connected black island, compute its outside perimeter using orthogonal edges. A perimeter of 4 becomes color 3, a perimeter of 6 becomes color 2, and a perimeter of 8 becomes color 1. Recolor the whole island with that color.

Describing the input grid: A 10x10 grid containing grey cells and black empty cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same 10x10 size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep every grey cell unchanged. Find each 4-connected black component. Recolor the black cells by the size of their component: isolated 1-cell components become green 3, 2-cell components become red 2, and 3-cell components become blue 1.

Describing the input grid: A 10x10 grid where black cells form small orthogonally connected groups inside a grey background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains 10x10.
Describing how to transform the grid: Leave grey cells unchanged. For each 4-connected group of black cells, count the number of shared orthogonal adjacencies between black cells inside that group. If the group has 0 internal adjacencies, recolor it green 3; if it has 1 internal adjacency, recolor it red 2; if it has 2 internal adjacencies, recolor it blue 1.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5x11 colored masks, one above and one below a grey divider row, with black as empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 5x11.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each position, compare the cell in the upper panel with the cell in the same relative position in the lower panel; output black if both are black or both are colored, otherwise output the single colored value present at that position.

Describing the input grid: The input is an 11x11 grid split by a horizontal grey divider row into a 5x11 upper panel and a 5x11 lower panel.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 5x11.
Describing how to transform the grid: Remove the grey divider and overlay the lower 5x11 panel directly onto the upper 5x11 panel without shifting horizontally; black cells are transparent, and if both panels have a colored cell in the same position, keep the upper panel’s color.

candidate_results.program:
import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)

    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]

    for c in colors:
        coords = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if coords.size == 0:
            continue

        rows = coords[:, 0]
        cols = coords[:, 1]
        rmin, rmax = rows.min(), rows.max()
        cmin, cmax = cols.min(), cols.max()

        # Copy all cells of the object first
        out[grid == c] = c

        # Shift top border one cell right
        top_rows = np.where(rows == rmin)[0]
        for idx in top_rows:
            r, col = coords[idx]
            if col + 1 < w:
                out[r, col] = 0
                out[r, col + 1] = c

        # Shift left border one cell right
        left_cols = np.where(cols == cmin)[0]
        for idx in left_cols:
            r, col = coords[idx]
            if col + 1 < w:
                out[r, col] = 0
                out[r, col + 1] = c

        # Ensure bottom and right borders remain as in original
        # Repaint original bottom and right border cells
        bottom_rows = np.where(rows == rmax)[0]
        for idx in bottom_rows:
            r, col = coords[idx]
            out[r, col] = c

        right_cols = np.where(cols == cmax)[0]
        for idx in right_cols:
            r, col = coords[idx]
            out[r, col] = c

    return out

candidate_results.program:
import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)

    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]

    for c in colors:
        coords = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if coords.size == 0:
            continue

        rows = coords[:, 0]
        cols = coords[:, 1]
        rmin, rmax = rows.min(), rows.max()
        cmin, cmax = cols.min(), cols.max()

        # Determine the shape's bounding box and shift the top and left borders right by 1.
        # We reconstruct by moving every cell that belongs to the top border or left border
        # one step right, while keeping the bottom and right borders fixed.
        shape = (grid == c)

        # Identify border cells by local neighborhood within the color mask.
        # A cell is on the border if any 4-neighbor is not the same color or out of bounds.
        border = np.zeros_like(shape, dtype=bool)
        for r, cc in coords:
            if r == 0 or r == h - 1 or cc == 0 or cc == w - 1:
                border[r, cc] = True
                continue
            if (grid[r - 1, cc] != c or grid[r + 1, cc] != c or
                grid[r, cc - 1] != c or grid[r, cc + 1] != c):
                border[r, cc] = True

        # Classify border cells into top/left/bottom/right using their position relative to bbox.
        # Top border: cells on the topmost row of the object.
        # Left border: cells on the leftmost column of the object.
        # Bottom/right borders remain unchanged.
        for r, cc in coords:
            if not border[r, cc]:
                continue
            nr, nc = r, cc
            if r == rmin or cc == cmin:
                nc = min(cc + 1, w - 1)
            out[nr, nc] = c

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)

    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]

    for c in colors:
        coords = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if coords.size == 0:
            continue

        rows = coords[:, 0]
        cols = coords[:, 1]
        bottom = rows.max()

        # Keep the lowest occupied row unchanged
        bottom_cols = cols[rows == bottom]
        out[bottom, bottom_cols] = c

        # For rows above the bottom, shift the occupied span one cell right
        for r in np.unique(rows[rows < bottom]):
            rc = cols[rows == r]
            if rc.size == 0:
                continue
            left = rc.min()
            right = rc.max()
            new_left = left + 1
            new_right = right
            if new_left <= new_right:
                out[r, new_left:new_right + 1] = c

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    g = input_grid
    h, w = g.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(g)

    pts = np.argwhere(g != 0)
    if len(pts) != 2:
        return out

    (r1, c1), (r2, c2) = pts
    v1, v2 = g[r1, c1], g[r2, c2]

    # Determine orientation: same row => vertical stripes (columns)
    # same column => horizontal stripes (rows)
    if r1 == r2:
        # Column clues: fill full columns, alternating colors, starting from earlier clue position
        c_start, c_end = sorted([c1, c2])
        step = c_end - c_start
        if step == 0:
            step = 1
        cols = []
        c = c_start
        while c < w:
            cols.append(c)
            c += step
        for i, col in enumerate(cols):
            out[:, col] = v1 if i % 2 == 0 else v2
    else:
        # Row clues: fill full rows, alternating colors, starting from earlier clue position
        r_start, r_end = sorted([r1, r2])
        step = r_end - r_start
        if step == 0:
            step = 1
        rows = []
        r = r_start
        while r < h:
            rows.append(r)
            r += step
        for i, row in enumerate(rows):
            out[row, :] = v1 if i % 2 == 0 else v2

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    g = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = g.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(g)

    pts = np.argwhere(g != 0)
    if len(pts) != 2:
        return out

    (r1, c1), (r2, c2) = pts
    v1, v2 = int(g[r1, c1]), int(g[r2, c2])

    # Determine orientation by whether clues are on top/bottom borders or left/right borders.
    top_bottom = (r1 in (0, h - 1)) or (r2 in (0, h - 1))
    left_right = (c1 in (0, w - 1)) or (c2 in (0, w - 1))

    if top_bottom and not left_right:
        # Vertical stripes: fill full columns, repeating to the right from the leftmost clue.
        cols = sorted([c1, c2])
        start = cols[0]
        spacing = abs(c2 - c1)
        if spacing == 0:
            spacing = 1
        x = start
        toggle = 0
        while x < w:
            out[:, x] = v1 if toggle == 0 else v2
            x += spacing
            toggle ^= 1
    else:
        # Horizontal stripes: fill full rows, repeating downward from the topmost clue.
        rows = sorted([r1, r2])
        start = rows[0]
        spacing = abs(r2 - r1)
        if spacing == 0:
            spacing = 1
        y = start
        toggle = 0
        while y < h:
            out[y, :] = v1 if toggle == 0 else v2
            y += spacing
            toggle ^= 1

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    nz = np.argwhere(grid != 0)
    if nz.size == 0:
        return grid

    colors = [grid[r, c] for r, c in nz]
    if h > w:
        # horizontal stripes from seed rows
        rows = sorted([(r, c, grid[r, c]) for r, c in nz], key=lambda x: x[0])
        r1, _, c1 = rows[0]
        r2, _, c2 = rows[1]
        out = np.zeros_like(grid)
        start = min(r1, r2)
        step = abs(r2 - r1)
        if step == 0:
            step = 1
        pattern = [grid[r1, c1], grid[r2, c2]]
        idx = 0
        for r in range(start, h, step):
            out[r, :] = pattern[idx % 2]
            idx += 1
        return out
    else:
        # vertical stripes from seed columns
        cols = sorted([(r, c, grid[r, c]) for r, c in nz], key=lambda x: x[1])
        _, c1, _ = cols[0]
        _, c2, _ = cols[1]
        r1, _, col1 = cols[0]
        r2, _, col2 = cols[1]
        out = np.zeros_like(grid)
        start = min(c1, c2)
        step = abs(c2 - c1)
        if step == 0:
            step = 1

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    g = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = g.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(g)

    pts = np.argwhere(g != 0)
    if len(pts) != 2:
        return out

    (r1, c1), (r2, c2) = pts
    v1, v2 = int(g[r1, c1]), int(g[r2, c2])

    # Determine orientation by whether clues are on top/bottom borders or left/right borders.
    top_bottom = (r1 in (0, h - 1)) or (r2 in (0, h - 1))
    left_right = (c1 in (0, w - 1)) or (c2 in (0, w - 1))

    if top_bottom and not left_right:
        # Vertical stripes: fill full columns, repeating to the right from the leftmost clue.
        cols = sorted([c1, c2])
        start = cols[0]
        spacing = abs(c2 - c1)
        if spacing == 0:
            spacing = 1
        x = start
        toggle = 0
        while x < w:
            out[:, x] = v1 if toggle == 0 else v2
            x += spacing
            toggle ^= 1
    else:
        # Horizontal stripes: fill full rows, repeating downward from the topmost clue.
        rows = sorted([r1, r2])
        start = rows[0]
        spacing = abs(r2 - r1)
        if spacing == 0:
            spacing = 1
        y = start
        toggle = 0
        while y < h:
            out[y, :] = v1 if toggle == 0 else v2
            y += spacing
            toggle ^= 1

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    g = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = g.shape

    # Find all-black separator rows/cols
    black_rows = np.where(np.all(g == 0, axis=1))[0]
    black_cols = np.where(np.all(g == 0, axis=0))[0]

    # Split into contiguous non-black row blocks
    row_blocks = []
    start = 0
    for r in black_rows:
        if r > start:
            row_blocks.append((start, r))
        start = r + 1
    if start < h:
        row_blocks.append((start, h))

    # Split into contiguous non-black col blocks
    col_blocks = []
    start = 0
    for c in black_cols:
        if c > start:
            col_blocks.append((start, c))
        start = c + 1
    if start < w:
        col_blocks.append((start, w))

    # Expect a 2x2 arrangement; extract panels
    panels = []
    for rs, re in row_blocks:
        row_panels = []
        for cs, ce in col_blocks:
            panel = g[rs:re, cs:ce]
            row_panels.append(panel)
        panels.append(row_panels)

    # Determine the shared color of the two top panels
    def nonzero_color(panel):
        vals = panel[panel != 0]
        if vals.size == 0:
            return 0
        uniq = np.unique(vals)
        return int(uniq[0])

    top_left = panels[0][0]
    top_right = panels[0][1]
    bottom_left = panels[1][0]
    bottom_right = panels[1][1]

    ref_color = nonzero_color(top_left)
    # top panels share same non-black color; if not, use the other top panel's color
    if ref_color == 0:
        ref_color = nonzero_color(top_right)

    bl_color = nonzero_color(bottom_left)
    br_color = nonzero_color(bottom_right)

    # Select the bottom panel whose non-black color differs from the reference color
    if bl_color != ref_color:
        return bottom_left.copy()
    return bottom_right.copy()

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape

    # Find row templates by grouping compatible rows and merging their non-zero cells.
    templates = []
    row_to_template = [-1] * h

    for i in range(h):
        row = grid[i]
        assigned = False
        for t_idx, tmpl in enumerate(templates):
            # Compatible if all overlapping non-zero cells agree
            mask = (row != 0) & (tmpl != 0)
            if np.all(row[mask] == tmpl[mask]):
                templates[t_idx] = np.where(row != 0, row, tmpl)
                row_to_template[i] = t_idx
                assigned = True
                break
        if not assigned:
            templates.append(row.copy())
            row_to_template[i] = len(templates) - 1

    # Propagate merged templates back to all rows
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)
    for i in range(h):
        out[i] = templates[row_to_template[i]]

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape

    # Find the smallest repeating row period by exact row equality on non-erased patterns.
    # Since rows are repeated templates with some black cells, rows belonging to the same
    # template are compatible and can be merged.
    row_signatures = []
    for r in range(h):
        row = grid[r]
        sig = tuple(row.tolist())
        row_signatures.append(sig)

    # Group rows by compatibility: rows are compatible if they never conflict on non-zero cells.
    groups = []
    used = [False] * h

    for i in range(h):
        if used[i]:
            continue
        group = [i]
        used[i] = True
        template = grid[i].copy()
        changed = True
        while changed:
            changed = False
            for j in range(h):
                if used[j]:
                    continue
                row = grid[j]
                ok = True
                for c in range(w):
                    a = template[c]
                    b = row[c]
                    if a != 0 and b != 0 and a != b:
                        ok = False
                        break
                if ok:
                    # merge
                    for c in range(w):
                        if template[c] == 0 and row[c] != 0:
                            template[c] = row[c]
                    group.append(j)
                    used[j] = True
                    changed = True
        groups.append((group, template))

    # Reconstruct each row by its group's merged template.
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)
    for group, template in groups:
        for r in group:
            out[r] = template

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape

    cols = [grid[:, j].copy() for j in range(w)]
    groups = []
    assigned = [False] * w

    for j in range(w):
        if assigned[j]:
            continue
        group = [j]
        assigned[j] = True
        for k in range(j + 1, w):
            if assigned[k]:
                continue
            a = cols[j]
            b = cols[k]
            mask = (a != 0) & (b != 0)
            if np.all(a[mask] == b[mask]):
                group.append(k)
                assigned[k] = True
        groups.append(group)

    out = np.zeros_like(grid)
    for group in groups:
        template = np.zeros(h, dtype=grid.dtype)
        for j in group:
            col = cols[j]
            template = np.where((template == 0) & (col != 0), col, template)
        for j in group:
            out[:, j] = template

    return out

                out[r, c] = 8
                out[r + 1, c] = 8
                out[r, c + 1] = 8
                out[r + 1, c + 1] = 8

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros((h, w), dtype=grid.dtype)

    # Fill red columns
    red_cols = np.where(np.any(grid == 2, axis=0))[0]
    for c in red_cols:
        out[:, c] = 2

    # Fill blue/green rows, overriding red where they intersect
    for r in range(h):
        row_vals = grid[r]
        if np.any(row_vals == 1):
            out[r, :] = 1
        elif np.any(row_vals == 3):
            out[r, :] = 3

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)

    odd_colors = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9]
    even_colors = [2, 4, 6, 8]

    # Draw horizontal lines for odd colors
    for c in odd_colors:
        rows = np.where(np.any(grid == c, axis=1))[0]
        for r in rows:
            out[r, :] = c

    # Draw vertical lines for even colors, but do not overwrite odd horizontal lines
    for c in even_colors:
        cols = np.where(np.any(grid == c, axis=0))[0]
        for col in cols:
            mask = out[:, col] == 0
            out[mask, col] = c

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros((h, w), dtype=grid.dtype)

    # Red markers (2) select columns
    col_mask = np.any(grid == 2, axis=0)
    out[:, col_mask] = 2

    # Non-red markers select rows, with row color priority
    for r in range(h):

        nonzero = vals[(vals != 0) & (vals != 2)]
        if nonzero.size > 0:
            color = int(nonzero[0])
            out[r, :] = color

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = grid.shape

    # Find the monochrome rectangular frame color and its bounding box
    best = None  # (area, top, left, bottom, right, color)
    colors = np.unique(grid)
    for c in colors:
        if c == 0:
            continue
        pos = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if pos.size == 0:
            continue
        top, left = pos.min(axis=0)
        bottom, right = pos.max(axis=0)
        sub = grid[top:bottom + 1, left:right + 1]
        # Check if border is monochrome c
        if sub.shape[0] >= 2 and sub.shape[1] >= 2:
            if (np.all(sub[0, :] == c) and np.all(sub[-1, :] == c) and
                np.all(sub[:, 0] == c) and np.all(sub[:, -1] == c)):
                area = sub.shape[0] * sub.shape[1]
                if best is None or area > best[0]:
                    best = (area, top, left, bottom, right, c)

    if best is None:
        return np.zeros((0, 0), dtype=grid.dtype)

    _, top, left, bottom, right, c = best
    return grid[top + 1:bottom, left + 1:right]

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = grid.shape

    # Find the color that forms the largest connected hollow rectangle border.
    # Heuristic: choose the color with the largest number of cells that also
    # appears to form a rectangular frame (many cells on a bounding box border).
    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]
    best = None
    best_score = -1
    best_bbox = None

    for c in colors:
        ys, xs = np.where(grid == c)
        if len(ys) == 0:
            continue
        y0, y1 = ys.min(), ys.max()
        x0, x1 = xs.min(), xs.max()
        bbox = grid[y0:y1+1, x0:x1+1]
        if bbox.size == 0:
            continue

        border = np.zeros_like(bbox, dtype=bool)
        border[0, :] = True
        border[-1, :] = True
        border[:, 0] = True
        border[:, -1] = True
        border_count = np.sum(bbox[border] == c)
        total_count = np.sum(grid == c)

        # Prefer colors that strongly occupy the border of their bbox.
        score = border_count * 1000 + total_count
        if score > best_score:
            best_score = score
            best = c
            best_bbox = (y0, y1, x0, x1)

    if best_bbox is None:
        return grid.copy()

    y0, y1, x0, x1 = best_bbox

    # Crop to the interior of the rectangle border.
    if y1 - y0 < 2 or x1 - x0 < 2:
        return np.zeros((0, 0), dtype=grid.dtype)

    return grid[y0 + 1:y1, x0 + 1:x1]

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = grid.shape

    # Find the color that forms the largest connected hollow rectangle border.
    # We identify candidate colors by their total count and look for a bounding box
    # whose perimeter is mostly/entirely that color.
    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]

    best = None
    best_score = -1
    best_box = None

    for c in colors:
        ys, xs = np.where(grid == c)
        if len(ys) == 0:
            continue
        y0, y1 = ys.min(), ys.max()
        x0, x1 = xs.min(), xs.max()
        if y1 <= y0 or x1 <= x0:
            continue

        box = grid[y0:y1 + 1, x0:x1 + 1]
        bh, bw = box.shape
        if bh < 3 or bw < 3:
            continue

        perimeter_mask = np.zeros_like(box, dtype=bool)
        perimeter_mask[0, :] = True
        perimeter_mask[-1, :] = True
        perimeter_mask[:, 0] = True
        perimeter_mask[:, -1] = True

        perimeter = box[perimeter_mask]
        score = np.sum(perimeter == c)

        # Prefer colors that dominate the perimeter and have a large bounding box.
        total_perim = perimeter.size
        if score > best_score or (score == best_score and total_perim > (0 if best_box is None else best_box[0] * best_box[1])):
            best_score = score
            best = c
            best_box = (bh, bw, y0, y1, x0, x1)

    if best_box is None:
        return grid.copy()

    bh, bw, y0, y1, x0, x1 = best_box

    # Crop the interior of the rectangle, removing the outer border.
    out = grid[y0 + 1:y1, x0 + 1:x1].copy()
    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = grid.shape

    # Find the most frequent non-zero color that forms the rectangular frame
    vals, counts = np.unique(grid[grid != 0], return_counts=True)
    if len(vals) == 0:
        return grid.copy()
    frame_color = vals[np.argmax(counts)]

    # Rows and columns containing the frame color
    rows = np.where(np.any(grid == frame_color, axis=1))[0]
    cols = np.where(np.any(grid == frame_color, axis=0))[0]

    # Determine top/bottom horizontal segments and left/right vertical sides
    # Use the outermost rows/cols containing the frame color
    top = rows.min()
    bottom = rows.max()
    left = cols.min()
    right = cols.max()

    # Extract interior excluding the delimiter cells themselves
    return grid[top + 1:bottom, left + 1:right].copy()

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    h, w = grid.shape

    # Find the monochrome rectangular frame color and its bounding box
    best = None  # (area, top, left, bottom, right, color)
    colors = np.unique(grid)
    for c in colors:
        if c == 0:
            continue
        pos = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if pos.size == 0:
            continue
        top, left = pos.min(axis=0)
        bottom, right = pos.max(axis=0)
        sub = grid[top:bottom + 1, left:right + 1]
        # Check if border is monochrome c
        if sub.shape[0] >= 2 and sub.shape[1] >= 2:
            if (np.all(sub[0, :] == c) and np.all(sub[-1, :] == c) and
                np.all(sub[:, 0] == c) and np.all(sub[:, -1] == c)):
                area = sub.shape[0] * sub.shape[1]
                if best is None or area > best[0]:
                    best = (area, top, left, bottom, right, c)

    if best is None:
        return np.zeros((0, 0), dtype=grid.dtype)

    _, top, left, bottom, right, c = best
    return grid[top + 1:bottom, left + 1:right]

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, dtype=int)
    n, m = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)

    # Find the 2x2 marker and the non-red color
    color = None
    top = left = None

    for r in range(n - 1):
        for c in range(m - 1):
            block = grid[r:r+2, c:c+2]
            vals = block.flatten()
            if np.count_nonzero(vals) == 4 and np.all(vals == vals[0]):
                continue
            # Look for a 2x2 block containing exactly one red (2) and three same nonzero color
            if np.count_nonzero(block == 2) == 1:
                nonzero = vals[vals != 0]
                if len(nonzero) == 4:
                    uniq = np.unique(nonzero)
                    if len(uniq) == 2 and 2 in uniq:
                        other = uniq[uniq != 2]
                        if len(other) == 1:
                            color = int(other[0])
                            top, left = r, c
                            break
        if color is not None:
            break

    if color is None:
        return out

    block = grid[top:top+2, left:left+2]
    red_pos = np.argwhere(block == 2)[0]
    rr, cc = int(red_pos[0]), int(red_pos[1])

    # Determine direction from red corner
    dr = -1 if rr == 0 else 1
    dc = -1 if cc == 0 else 1

    # Starting 2x2 square is the marker area filled with color
    # Copy it repeatedly along the diagonal direction
    k = 0
    while True:
        r0 = top + k * dr
        c0 = left + k * dc
        r1 = r0 + 1
        c1 = c0 + 1

        if r1 < 0 or c1 < 0 or r0 >= n or c0 >= m:
            break

        rs = max(r0, 0)
        re = min(r1, n - 1)
        cs = max(c0, 0)
        ce = min(c1, m - 1)
        out[rs:re+1, cs:ce+1] = color

        k += 1

    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    g = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = g.shape
    nz = np.argwhere(g != 0)
    if nz.size == 0:
        return g

    # Find the 2x2 pattern region: minimal bounding box of nonzero cells
    r0, c0 = nz.min(axis=0)
    r1, c1 = nz.max(axis=0)

    # Identify the color C and the red marker cells inside the 2x2 block
    # The block is assumed to be within the bounding box and may contain one or more nonzero cells.
    # Color C is the non-red color present in the block.
    block = g[r0:r1 + 1, c0:c1 + 1]
    vals = block[block != 0]
    if vals.size == 0:
        return g

    # Prefer the most frequent nonzero color as C
    uniq, cnt = np.unique(vals, return_counts=True)
    c = int(uniq[np.argmax(cnt)])

    # Determine the 2x2 square location by finding a 2x2 area containing c and/or red markers.
    # Search all 2x2 windows for one that contains at least one c and at least one nonzero.
    best = None
    for r in range(max(0, r0 - 1), min(h - 1, r1 + 1)):
        for col in range(max(0, c0 - 1), min(w - 1, c1 + 1)):
            win = g[r:r + 2, col:col + 2]
            if win.shape != (2, 2):
                continue
            if np.any(win != 0):
                if np.any(win == c):
                    best = (r, col)
                    break
        if best is not None:
            break

    if best is None:
        return g

    r, col = best

    # Determine which corners are red markers
    corners = {
        "tl": g[r, col],
        "tr": g[r, col + 1],
        "bl": g[r + 1, col],
        "br": g[r + 1, col + 1],
    }

    # Solid 2x2 square of color C
    g[r:r + 2, col:col + 2] = c

    # For each red corner, extend the three diagonals of the solid square outward
    # through that corner's diagonal direction until the boundary.
    def draw_diag(start_r, start_c, dr, dc):
        rr, cc = start_r, start_c
        while 0 <= rr < h and 0 <= cc < w:
            g[rr, cc] = c
            rr += dr
            cc += dc

    if corners["tl"] != 0 and corners["tl"] != c:
        draw_diag(r, col, -1, -1)
        draw_diag(r, col + 1, -1, 0)
        draw_diag(r + 1, col, 0, -1)

    if corners["tr"] != 0 and corners["tr"] != c:
        draw_diag(r, col + 1, -1, 1)
        draw_diag(r, col, -1, 0)
        draw_diag(r + 1, col + 1, 0, 1)

    if corners["bl"] != 0 and corners["bl"] != c:
        draw_diag(r + 1, col, 1, -1)
        draw_diag(r, col, 0, -1)
        draw_diag(r + 1, col + 1, 1, 0)

    if corners["br"] != 0 and corners["br"] != c:
        draw_diag(r + 1, col + 1, 1, 1)
        draw_diag(r, col + 1, 0, 1)
        draw_diag(r + 1, col, 1, 0)

    return g

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    vals, counts = np.unique(grid[grid != 0], return_counts=True)
    if len(vals) == 0:

    coords = np.argwhere(grid == target)

    return grid[r0:r1, c0:c1]

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    vals, counts = np.unique(grid[grid != 0], return_counts=True)
    if len(vals) == 0:
        return np.zeros((0, 0), dtype=grid.dtype)
    target = vals[np.argmin(counts)]
    mask = grid == target
    rows = np.where(mask.any(axis=1))[0]
    cols = np.where(mask.any(axis=0))[0]
    return grid[rows.min():rows.max() + 1, cols.min():cols.max() + 1]

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    out = grid.copy()
    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]
    for c in colors:
        pts = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if len(pts) != 2:
            continue
        (r1, col1), (r2, col2) = pts
        dr = 1 if r2 > r1 else -1
        dc = 1 if col2 > col1 else -1
        r, col = r1, col1
        while True:
            out[r, col] = c
            if r == r2 and col == col2:
                break
            r += dr
            col += dc
    return out

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    n, m = grid.shape
    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]

        pts = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if len(pts) != 2:
            continue
        (r1, col1), (r2, col2) = pts
        dr = np.sign(r2 - r1)
        dc = np.sign(col2 - col1)
        r, col = r1, col1
        while True:
            grid[r, col] = c
            if r == r2 and col == col2:
                break
            r += dr
            col += dc

    return grid

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]
    
    for c in colors:
        positions = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if len(positions) < 2:
            continue
        # Use the two markers for this color
        (r1, col1), (r2, col2) = positions[0], positions[1]
        dr = 1 if r2 > r1 else -1
        dc = 1 if col2 > col1 else -1
        
        r, col = r1, col1
        grid[r, col] = c
        while (r, col) != (r2, col2):
            r += dr
            col += dc
            if 0 <= r < h and 0 <= col < w:
                grid[r, col] = c
    return grid

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    n, m = grid.shape
    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]

    for c in colors:
        pts = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if len(pts) != 2:
            continue
        (r1, col1), (r2, col2) = pts
        dr = np.sign(r2 - r1)
        dc = np.sign(col2 - col1)
        r, col = r1, col1
        while True:
            grid[r, col] = c
            if r == r2 and col == col2:
                break
            r += dr
            col += dc

    return grid

candidate_results.program:
import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)
    colors = [c for c in np.unique(grid) if c != 0]

    for c in colors:
        coords = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if coords.size == 0:
            continue

        rows = coords[:, 0]
        cols = coords[:, 1]
        rmin, rmax = rows.min(), rows.max()
        cmin, cmax = cols.min(), cols.max()

        # Determine symmetry axis from bounding box
        axis2 = cmin + cmax  # twice the axis position
        # For each row, fill horizontal spans between symmetric pairs
        for r in range(rmin, rmax + 1):
            row_cols = np.where(grid[r] == c)[0]
            if row_cols.size == 0:
                continue
            row_set = set(row_cols.tolist())
            used = set()
            for x in row_cols:
                if x in used:
                    continue
                y2 = axis2 - 2 * x
                # y = axis2 - x, but using integers directly
                y = axis2 - x
                if y in row_set:
                    left, right = (x, y) if x <= y else (y, x)
                    out[r, left:right + 1] = c
                    used.add(x)
                    used.add(y)
                else:
                    # If no exact symmetric partner, preserve the cell
                    out[r, x] = c

    return out

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    rows, cols = grid.shape

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)
    rows, cols = np.where(grid != 0)
    if rows.size == 0:
        return out
    out_rows = rows + 1
    valid = out_rows < grid.shape[0]
    out[out_rows[valid], cols[valid]] = grid[rows[valid], cols[valid]]
    return out

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape
    out = np.zeros_like(grid)

    out[ys + 1, xs] = color
    return out

candidate_results.program:
import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    n = input_grid.shape[0]
    out = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
    color = 3
    r = c = 0
    out[r, c] = color

    lengths = []
    L = n - 1
    while L > 0:
        lengths.extend([L, L])
        L -= 2

    dirs = [(0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1), (-1, 0)]
    d = 0
    for L in lengths:
        dr, dc = dirs[d % 4]
        for _ in range(L):
            r += dr
            c += dc
            if 0 <= r < n and 0 <= c < n:
                out[r, c] = color
        d += 1

    return out

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    n, m = input_grid.shape

        right -= 1

        out[top:bottom + 1, left] = color
        left += 1

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    n = input_grid.shape[0]
    out = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
    color = 3

    def draw(r0, c0, size):
        if size <= 0:
            return
        r1 = r0 + size - 1
        c1 = c0 + size - 1

        out[r0, c0:c1 + 1] = color
        out[r1, c0:c1 + 1] = color
        out[r0:r1 + 1, c1] = color
        if size > 1:
            out[r0 + 1:r1 + 1, c0] = color
            out[r0 + 1, c0] = 0

        if r0 + 2 <= r1 and c0 + 1 <= c1:
            out[r0 + 2, c0 + 1] = color

        draw(r0 + 2, c0 + 2, size - 4)

    draw(0, 0, n)
    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    n = input_grid.shape[0]
    out = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
    color = 3

    def draw_layer(top, left, size):
        if size <= 0:
            return
        bottom = top + size - 1
        right = left + size - 1

        out[top, left:right + 1] = color
        out[bottom, left:right + 1] = color
        out[top:bottom + 1, right] = color

        if bottom - top >= 1:
            out[top + 1:bottom + 1, left] = color
            out[top + 1, left] = 0

        if top + 2 <= bottom and left + 1 <= right:
            out[top + 2, left + 1] = color

    top = 0
    left = 0
    size = n
    while size > 0:
        draw_layer(top, left, size)
        top += 2
        left += 2
        size -= 4

    return out

        bottom -= 1

                nr, nc = r + dr, c + dc
                if 0 <= nr < n and 0 <= nc < n:
                    r, c = nr, nc
                    out[r, c] = 0
            d += 1
            if d % 2 == 0:
                step -= 2
            else:
                step -= 1
    
    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    n = input_grid.shape[0]
    out = np.zeros((n, n), dtype=int)
    color = 3

    def draw(r0, c0, size):
        if size <= 0:
            return
        r1 = r0 + size - 1
        c1 = c0 + size - 1

        out[r0, c0:c1 + 1] = color
        out[r1, c0:c1 + 1] = color
        out[r0:r1 + 1, c1] = color
        if size > 1:
            out[r0 + 1:r1 + 1, c0] = color
            out[r0 + 1, c0] = 0

        if r0 + 2 <= r1 and c0 + 1 <= c1:
            out[r0 + 2, c0 + 1] = color

        draw(r0 + 2, c0 + 2, size - 4)

    draw(0, 0, n)
    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape

    vals, counts = np.unique(grid, return_counts=True)
    bg = vals[np.argmax(counts)]

    # Find the non-background color that forms the largest connected rectangular block
    # by taking the most frequent non-bg color.
    non_bg_vals = vals[vals != bg]
    if len(non_bg_vals) == 0:
        return grid
    obj_color = non_bg_vals[np.argmax([np.sum(grid == v) for v in non_bg_vals])]

    coords = np.argwhere(grid == obj_color)
    r0, c0 = coords.min(axis=0)
    r1, c1 = coords.max(axis=0)

    # Determine rectangle bounds by expanding to include all contiguous obj_color cells
    # assuming a solid rectangle.
    # Since the rectangle is solid, its bounds are the min/max coordinates.
    top, left, bottom, right = r0, c0, r1, c1

    # Identify isolated markers: non-bg, non-rectangle-color single cells of other colors.
    # For each such cell, if aligned with rectangle, draw line to nearest edge.
    for color in vals:
        if color == bg or color == obj_color:
            continue
        positions = np.argwhere(grid == color)
        for r, c in positions:
            if top <= r <= bottom:
                if c < left:
                    grid[r, c:left] = np.where(grid[r, c:left] == bg, color, grid[r, c:left])
                elif c > right:
                    grid[r, right + 1:c + 1] = np.where(grid[r, right + 1:c + 1] == bg, color, grid[r, right + 1:c + 1])
            elif left <= c <= right:
                if r < top:
                    grid[r:top, c] = np.where(grid[r:top, c] == bg, color, grid[r:top, c])
                elif r > bottom:
                    grid[bottom + 1:r + 1, c] = np.where(grid[bottom + 1:r + 1, c] == bg, color, grid[bottom + 1:r + 1, c])

    return grid

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape

    vals, counts = np.unique(grid, return_counts=True)
    bg = vals[np.argmax(counts)]

    # Find the non-background color that forms the largest connected rectangular block.
    # Since the task describes one solid rectangle plus isolated markers, we can detect
    # the rectangle color as the most frequent non-background color.
    non_bg_vals = vals[vals != bg]
    if len(non_bg_vals) == 0:
        return grid
    rect_color = non_bg_vals[np.argmax([np.sum(grid == v) for v in non_bg_vals])]

    rect_positions = np.argwhere(grid == rect_color)
    if rect_positions.size == 0:
        return grid

    rmin, cmin = rect_positions.min(axis=0)
    rmax, cmax = rect_positions.max(axis=0)

    # Determine exact rectangle bounds by expanding to the full filled rectangle.
    # Use the bounding box of rect_color cells.
    rect_rows = np.where(np.any(grid == rect_color, axis=1))[0]
    rect_cols = np.where(np.any(grid == rect_color, axis=0))[0]
    if len(rect_rows) == 0 or len(rect_cols) == 0:
        return grid
    rmin, rmax = rect_rows[0], rect_rows[-1]
    cmin, cmax = rect_cols[0], rect_cols[-1]

    # Process isolated marker cells of colors other than background and rectangle color.
    marker_colors = [v for v in vals if v != bg and v != rect_color]
    for color in marker_colors:
        for r, c in np.argwhere(grid == color):
            # Same row as rectangle: draw horizontally to nearest rectangle edge.
            if rmin <= r <= rmax:
                if c < cmin:
                    grid[r, c+1:cmin] = color
                elif c > cmax:
                    grid[r, cmax+1:c] = color
            # Same column as rectangle: draw vertically to nearest rectangle edge.
            if cmin <= c <= cmax:
                if r < rmin:
                    grid[r+1:rmin, c] = color
                elif r > rmax:
                    grid[rmax+1:r, c] = color

    return grid

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = grid.shape

    vals, counts = np.unique(grid, return_counts=True)
    bg = vals[np.argmax(counts)]

    # Find the non-background color that forms the large rectangle
    colors = [v for v in vals if v != bg]
    if not colors:
        return grid
    rect_color = None
    rect_area = -1
    rect_bbox = None

    for c in colors:
        pos = np.argwhere(grid == c)
        if pos.size == 0:
            continue
        r0, c0 = pos.min(axis=0)
        r1, c1 = pos.max(axis=0)
        area = (r1 - r0 + 1) * (c1 - c0 + 1)
        cnt = len(pos)
        # Prefer a dense solid rectangle
        if area >= cnt and cnt > rect_area:
            rect_color = c
            rect_area = cnt
            rect_bbox = (r0, r1, c0, c1)

    if rect_color is None:
        return grid

    r0, r1, c0, c1 = rect_bbox

    # Extend markers along rows
    for r in range(h):
        row = grid[r]
        rect_cols = np.where(row == rect_color)[0]
        if rect_cols.size == 0:
            continue
        markers = np.where((row != bg) & (row != rect_color))[0]
        if markers.size == 0:
            continue
        left_rect = rect_cols.min()
        right_rect = rect_cols.max()
        for m in markers:
            mc = row[m]
            if m > right_rect:
                grid[r, right_rect + 1:m] = mc
            elif m < left_rect:
                grid[r, m + 1:left_rect] = mc

    # Extend markers along columns
    for c in range(w):
        col = grid[:, c]
        rect_rows = np.where(col == rect_color)[0]
        if rect_rows.size == 0:
            continue
        markers = np.where((col != bg) & (col != rect_color))[0]
        if markers.size == 0:
            continue
        top_rect = rect_rows.min()
        bot_rect = rect_rows.max()
        for m in markers:
            mc = col[m]
            if m > bot_rect:
                grid[bot_rect + 1:m, c] = mc
            elif m < top_rect:
                grid[m + 1:top_rect, c] = mc

    return grid

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:

    if not colors:
        return grid
    rect_color = max(colors, key=lambda c: np.sum(grid == c))

    marker_mask = (grid != bg) & (grid != rect_color)

        if rect_cols.size == 0 or marker_cols.size == 0:
            continue
        left, right = rect_cols.min(), rect_cols.max()
        for c in marker_cols:
            if c < left:
                grid[r, c:left] = grid[r, c]
            elif c > right:
                grid[r, right + 1:c + 1] = grid[r, c]
    
    # Recompute masks after row extensions
    rect_mask = grid == rect_color
    marker_mask = (grid != bg) & (grid != rect_color)
    
    # Extend markers along columns
    for c in range(w):
        rect_rows = np.where(rect_mask[:, c])[0]
        marker_rows = np.where(marker_mask[:, c])[0]
        if rect_rows.size == 0 or marker_rows.size == 0:
            continue
        top, bottom = rect_rows.min(), rect_rows.max()
        for r in marker_rows:
            if r < top:
                grid[r:top, c] = grid[r, c]
            elif r > bottom:
                grid[bottom + 1:r + 1, c] = grid[r, c]
    
    return grid

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    sep_rows = np.where(np.all(grid == 4, axis=1))[0]
    if len(sep_rows) == 0:
        return grid.copy()
    sep = sep_rows[0]
    top = grid[:sep]
    bottom = grid[sep + 1:]
    h = min(top.shape[0], bottom.shape[0])
    w = min(top.shape[1], bottom.shape[1])
    top = top[:h, :w]
    bottom = bottom[:h, :w]
    out = np.where((top == 2) ^ (bottom == 2), 3, 0)
    return out

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    grid = np.array(input_grid)
    sep_rows = np.where(np.all(grid == 4, axis=1))[0]
    if sep_rows.size == 0:
        return grid.copy()
    sep = sep_rows[0]
    top = grid[:sep]
    bottom = grid[sep + 1:]
    out = ((top == 2).astype(int) + (bottom == 2).astype(int)) % 2
    return out * 3

Describing the input grid: The input is a large grid divided by full separator lines into equal-sized square compartments; each compartment is either black or filled with one solid color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has exactly the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Work on the compartment-level grid. Find the demonstrated motif: one compartment of a “center” color is surrounded in certain neighboring offsets by compartments of a second color. Copy that same neighbor-offset pattern around every other compartment of the same center color, only adding the second color into black compartments and leaving separators and existing colored compartments unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a tiled board with separator lines, containing several isolated marker compartments of one color and nearby support compartments of another color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid remains the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Collapse the board into its compartments. Choose the isolated marker color, then for every pair of marker compartments, translate any neighboring support-color compartments found around one marker to the corresponding relative positions around the other marker. Add those translated support-color compartments wherever the target compartment is black and inside the board.

Describing how to transform the grid: Determine which one-step neighborhood positions around a seed compartment are already occupied by the fill color anywhere in the grid. Then apply this learned local rule to every seed compartment: every black compartment at one of those relative neighborhood positions becomes the fill color, while all existing colored cells and separator lines stay unchanged.

import numpy as np

def transform_grid(input_grid: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
    g = np.array(input_grid, copy=True)
    h, w = g.shape

    # Find separator color and cell size from full separator lines
    sep_color = None
    row_lines = []
    col_lines = []
    for c in range(w):
        vals = g[:, c]
        nz = vals[vals != 0]
        if len(nz) and np.all(nz == nz[0]) and np.sum(vals == nz[0]) == h:
            sep_color = int(nz[0])
            break
    if sep_color is None:
        return g

    row_lines = [r for r in range(h) if np.all(g[r, :] == sep_color)]
    col_lines = [c for c in range(w) if np.all(g[:, c] == sep_color)]

    # Cell boundaries are between separator lines
    row_bounds = [-1] + row_lines + [h]
    col_bounds = [-1] + col_lines + [w]

    cell_h = row_bounds[1] - row_bounds[0] - 1
    cell_w = col_bounds[1] - col_bounds[0] - 1

    # Extract cells
    cells = []
    for i in range(len(row_bounds) - 1):
        r0 = row_bounds[i] + 1
        r1 = row_bounds[i + 1]
        row = []
        for j in range(len(col_bounds) - 1):
            c0 = col_bounds[j] + 1
            c1 = col_bounds[j + 1]
            row.append(g[r0:r1, c0:c1])
        cells.append(row)

    cells = np.array(cells, dtype=object)
    nr, nc = cells.shape

    # Identify marker color: the non-separator color appearing in isolated cells
    colors = {}
    for i in range(nr):
        for j in range(nc):
            cell = cells[i, j]
            vals = cell[cell != 0]
            vals = vals[vals != sep_color]
            if len(vals):
                u, cnt = np.unique(vals, return_counts=True)
                for color, c in zip(u, cnt):
                    colors[int(color)] = colors.get(int(color), 0) + int(c)

    # Prefer colors that appear as solid blocks in cells
    def cell_nonzero_colors(cell):
        vals = cell[(cell != 0) & (cell != sep_color)]
        return set(map(int, np.unique(vals)))

    marker_color = None
    for color in sorted(colors, key=lambda x: -colors[x]):
        count_cells = 0
        for i in range(nr):
            for j in range(nc):
                if color in cell_nonzero_colors(cells[i, j]):
                    count_cells += 1
        if count_cells >= 2:
            marker_color = color
            break
    if marker_color is None:
        marker_color = max(colors, key=colors.get)

    # Find marker cells and support cells
    marker_cells = []
    support_cells = []
    for i in range(nr):
        for j in range(nc):
            cell = cells[i, j]
            nz = cell[(cell != 0) & (cell != sep_color)]
            if len(nz) == 0:
                continue
            uniq = set(map(int, np.unique(nz)))
            if uniq == {marker_color}:
                marker_cells.append((i, j))
            elif marker_color not in uniq:
                support_cells.append((i, j))

    # If no clear marker cells, return original
    if len(marker_cells) < 2:
        return g

    # Use first marker as source, second as target; propagate support pattern from source to target
    src = marker_cells[0]
    tgt = marker_cells[1]

    # Determine support pattern around source in cell coordinates
    pattern = {}
    for i, j in support_cells:
        di, dj = i - src[0], j - src[1]
        pattern[(di, dj)] = cells[i, j].copy()

    out = np.array(g, copy=True)

    # Clear target support area only where black inside cells
    for (di, dj), pat in pattern.items():
        ti, tj = tgt[0] + di, tgt[1] + dj
        if 0 <= ti < nr and 0 <= tj < nc:
            cell = out[row_bounds[ti] + 1:row_bounds[ti + 1], col_bounds[tj] + 1:col_bounds[tj + 1]]
            mask = (cell == 0)
            # place only nonzero, non-separator values from pattern
            p = pat.copy()
            p[(p == 0) | (p == sep_color)] = 0
            cell[mask & (p != 0)] = p[mask & (p != 0)]
            out[row_bounds[ti] + 1:row_bounds[ti + 1], col_bounds[tj] + 1:col_bounds[tj + 1]] = cell

    return out
